subject: Physics
q:
This question has Statement $-1$ and Statement $-2$. Of the four choices given after the statements, choose the one that best describes the two statements.
Statement-1 : Sky wave signals are used for long distance radio communication. These signals are in general, less stable than ground wave signals.
Statement-2 : The state of ionosphere varies from hour to hour, day to day and season to season.
question:
This question has Statement $-1$ and Statement $-2$. Of the four choices given after the statements, choose the one that best describes the two statements.
Statement-1 : Sky wave signals are used for long distance radio communication. These signals are in general, less stable than ground wave signals.
Statement-2 : The state of ionosphere varies from hour to hour, day to day and season to season.
options: Statement-1 is true, Statement-2 is true; Statement-2 is the correct explanation of Statement-1.
Statement-1 is true, Statement-2 is true; Statement-2 is not the correct explanation of Statement-1.
Statement- 1 is false, Statement- 2 is true.
Statement-1 is true, Statement-2 is false.
solution: Since ionospheric properties change with time, these signals are in general less stable than ground wave signals.

subject: Physics
q:
The amplitude of 15 sin(1000 πt) is modulated by 10 sin(4 πt) signal. The amplitude modulated signal contains frequencies of

A. 500 Hz

B. 2 Hz

C. 250 Hz

D. 498 Hz

E. 502 Hz

Choose the correct answer from the options given below
question:
The amplitude of 15 sin(1000 πt) is modulated by 10 sin(4 πt) signal. The amplitude modulated signal contains frequencies of

A. 500 Hz

B. 2 Hz

C. 250 Hz

D. 498 Hz

E. 502 Hz

Choose the correct answer from the options given below
solution:
The carrier wave frequency is given by

fc=1000π2π= 500 Hz

The modulating wave frequency is given by

fm=4π2π= 2 Hz

Hence, the frequencies contained in the modulated signal are given by

f=fc-fm,fm,fc+fm=498 Hz, 500 Hz, 502 Hz

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I : A speech signal of 2 kHz is used to modulate a carrier signal of 1 MHz. The bandwidth requirement for the signal is 4 kHz.

Statement II : The side band frequencies are 1002 kHz and 998 kHz. In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I : A speech signal of 2 kHz is used to modulate a carrier signal of 1 MHz. The bandwidth requirement for the signal is 4 kHz.

Statement II : The side band frequencies are 1002 kHz and 998 kHz. In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
options: Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
solution:
fm=2 kHz

fc=1 MHz=1000 kHz

Band width =2fm=4 kHz

∴ Side frequencies will be

=fc±fm

=1000±2 kHz

=998 kHz & 1002 kHz

So, statement-I & statement-II both are correct.

subject: Physics
q: An audio signal consists of two distinct sounds: one a human speech signal in the frequency band of 200 Hz to 2700 Hz, while the other is a high frequency music signal in the frequency band of 10200 Hz to 15200 Hz. The ratio of the AM signal band width required to send both the signals together to the AM signal band width required to send just the human speech is:
question: An audio signal consists of two distinct sounds: one a human speech signal in the frequency band of 200 Hz to 2700 Hz, while the other is a high frequency music signal in the frequency band of 10200 Hz to 15200 Hz. The ratio of the AM signal band width required to send both the signals together to the AM signal band width required to send just the human speech is:
solution:
Band width for Music signal =15200−10200 =5000 Hz

Band width for speech signal =2700−200=2500Hz

Band with for Both = 2500+5000=7500 Hz

Band with for Human speech =2500 Hz

Required Ratio =75002500=31

subject: Physics
q: A <math><mn>100</mn><mi>V</mi></math> carrier wave is made to vary between <math><mn>160</mn><mi> </mi><mi>V</mi></math> and <math><mn>40</mn><mi> </mi><mi>V</mi></math> by a modulating signal. What is the modulation index?
question: A <math><mn>100</mn><mi>V</mi></math> carrier wave is made to vary between <math><mn>160</mn><mi> </mi><mi>V</mi></math> and <math><mn>40</mn><mi> </mi><mi>V</mi></math> by a modulating signal. What is the modulation index?
solution:
AC=100

AC+Am=160

AC-Am=40

AC=100,Am=60

μ=AmAc=0.6

If an audio signal requires a bandwidth of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mi>kHz</mi></math>, how many channels can be accommodated for transmission
question: Only <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>%</mo></math> of the optical source frequency is the available channel bandwidth for an optical communicating system operating at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1000</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nm</mi></math>. If an audio signal requires a bandwidth of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mi>kHz</mi></math>, how many channels can be accommodated for transmission
solution:
Frequency f=cλ=3×1081000×10-9=3×1014 Hz

Band width used for transmission =2100×3×1014 Hz=6×1012 Hz

Number of channels N=Total bandwidth of channel Bandwidth needed per channel =6×10128×103=6008×107=75×107

subject: Physics
q: A baseband signal of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mi>MHz</mi></math> frequency is modulated with a carrier signal of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mi>GHz</mi></math> frequency using amplitude modulation method. What should be the minimum size of antenna required to transmit the modulated signal ?
question: A baseband signal of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mi>MHz</mi></math> frequency is modulated with a carrier signal of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mi>GHz</mi></math> frequency using amplitude modulation method. What should be the minimum size of antenna required to transmit the modulated signal ?
solution:
The wavelength of the carrier wave is,

λc=cνc=3×1083.5×109=335 m.

The minimum size of the antenna required is 14th of the wavelength of the carrier. Therefore,

l=14×335=21.4 mm

q: An amplitude-modulated wave is represented by, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>C</mi><mi>m</mi></msub><mo>(</mo><mi>t</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo>(</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>cos</mi><mn>12560</mn><mi>t</mi><mo>)</mo><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>111</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>4</mn></msup><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The modulating frequency in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>kHz</mi></math> will be
question: An amplitude-modulated wave is represented by, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>C</mi><mi>m</mi></msub><mo>(</mo><mi>t</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo>(</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>cos</mi><mn>12560</mn><mi>t</mi><mo>)</mo><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>111</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>4</mn></msup><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The modulating frequency in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>kHz</mi></math> will be
solution:
The function of the amplitude-modulated wave is represented by, Cm=AC1+AmACsin2πfmt, now compare it from the given equation, 
Cm(t)=10(1+0.2cos12560t)sin111×104t V

⇒2πfm=12560 s-1⇒fm=2000 Hz=2 kHz.

subject: Physics
q: For an amplitude modulated wave the minimum amplitude is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>, while the modulation index is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>%</mo></math>. The maximum amplitude of the modulated wave is:
question: For an amplitude modulated wave the minimum amplitude is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>, while the modulation index is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>%</mo></math>. The maximum amplitude of the modulated wave is:
solution:
Modulatio index is the ratio of the difference in amplitudes to the sum of the amplitudes.

The formula to calculate the modulation index of a wave is given by

μ=Amax-AminAmax+Amin   ...1

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (1) and solve to calculate the required value of maximum amplitude.

0.6=Amax-3Amax+3⇒0.6Amax+1.8=Amax-3⇒0.4Amax=4.8⇒Amax= 4.80.4=12 V

subject: Physics
q:
A transmitting antenna is kept on the surface of the earth. The minimum height of receiving antenna required to receive the signal in line of sight at 4 km distance from it is x×10-2 m. The value of x is _____.
(Let, radius of earth R=6400 km)
question:
A transmitting antenna is kept on the surface of the earth. The minimum height of receiving antenna required to receive the signal in line of sight at 4 km distance from it is x×10-2 m. The value of x is _____.
(Let, radius of earth R=6400 km)
solution:
The formula to calculate the range of the antenna is given by

γ=2Rh   ...1

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (1) and solve to obtain the required height of the antenna.

4×103=2×6400000×h⇒16×106=2×64×105×h⇒h=1602×64m=108m=10008×10-2 m=125×10-2 m

subject: Physics
q: If the sum of the heights of transmitting and receiving antennas in the line of sight of communication is fixed at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>160</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>,</mo></math> then the maximum range of LOS communication is _______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>km</mi></math> (Take radius of Earth <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>6400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math>)
question: If the sum of the heights of transmitting and receiving antennas in the line of sight of communication is fixed at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>160</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>,</mo></math> then the maximum range of LOS communication is _______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>km</mi></math> (Take radius of Earth <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>6400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math>)
solution:
hr+ht=160 (Given)
LOS=2Rhr+ht
LOS=2Rhr+160-hr
dLOSdhr=0=2R12hr-12160-hr=0
hr=ht=80 m
LOS=2R(80+80)=22×6400×103×80=64 km

If the height of the receiving antenna is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>70</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>,</mo></math> then the minimum height of the transmitting antenna should be: (Radius of the Earth <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>6</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>)

solution:
Range, d=2 RhT+2 Rhr
⇒50=2×6400×hT+2×6400×70×10-3
⇒2×6400×hT=50-814
hT=50-81422×6400=0.031458 km

subject: Physics
q: In a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>CE</mi></math> transistor amplifier, the audio signal voltage across the collector resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kΩ</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>, if the base resistance is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kΩ</mi></math> and the current amplification of the transistor is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn></math> then the input signal voltage is
question: In a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>CE</mi></math> transistor amplifier, the audio signal voltage across the collector resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kΩ</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>, if the base resistance is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kΩ</mi></math> and the current amplification of the transistor is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn></math> then the input signal voltage is
solution:
Current amplification factor,

β=ΔICΔIB

Collector current,

ΔIC=2V2×103Ω=1×10-3 A

Base current,

ΔIB=VBRB=VB1×103=VB×10-3

Given, β=100

Now, 100=10-3VB×10-3

⇒VB=1100 V=10 mV

q: A TV transmission tower has a height of <math><mn>140</mn><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi></math> and the height of the receiving antenna is <math><mn>40</mn><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi><mo>.</mo></math> What is the maximum distance upto which signals can be broadcasted from this tower in LOS (Line of Sight) mode? (Given: radius of earth <math><mo>=</mo><mn>6.4</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>.</mo></math>

solution:
D=2hTR+2hRR

D=2×140×64×105+2×40×64×105

D=8×103[28+8]

D=8× 10 3 [ 2 7 +2 2 ]

D=16× 10 3 [ 7+ 2 ]

D=16×1032.6+1.4

=64000m=64km≃65km

subject: Physics
q:
The height of a transmitting antenna at the top of a tower is 25 m and that of receiving antenna is, 49 m. The maximum distance between them, for satisfactory communication in LOS (Line-Of-Sight) is K5×102 m. The value of K is _____ .

(Assume radius of Earth is 64×105 m) [Calculate upto nearest integer value]
question:
The height of a transmitting antenna at the top of a tower is 25 m and that of receiving antenna is, 49 m. The maximum distance between them, for satisfactory communication in LOS (Line-Of-Sight) is K5×102 m. The value of K is _____ .

(Assume radius of Earth is 64×105 m) [Calculate upto nearest integer value]
solution:
Here, 

hT=49m, hR=25m, R=64×105m

Maximum distance between two antennae is,

d=2RhT+2RhR=2R49+2R25=122R=122×64×105=1925×102 m

subject: Physics
q: The required height of a TV tower which can cover the population of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>03</mn></math> lakh is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math>. If the average population density is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo></math>per square <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>km</mi></math> and the radius of earth is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math>, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math> will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>.
question: The required height of a TV tower which can cover the population of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>03</mn></math> lakh is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math>. If the average population density is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo></math>per square <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>km</mi></math> and the radius of earth is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math>, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math> will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>.
solution:
Maximum range of TV transmission d=2Rh

d=2×6400×h×10-3 (here, h in m)

Maximum area of TV transmission

Area =πd2

=π×2×6400×h×10-3 km2

Now, 6.03×100000=100×π×2×6400×10-3 h

h=6.03×10510×π×128

h=150 m

subject: Physics
q: A TV transmitting antenna is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>98</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> high and the receiving antenna is at the ground level. If the radius of the earth is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the surface area covered by the transmitting antenna is approximately:
question: A TV transmitting antenna is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>98</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> high and the receiving antenna is at the ground level. If the radius of the earth is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the surface area covered by the transmitting antenna is approximately:
solution:
The given data is 

H=98 m

R=6400 km

The line of sight formula for an antenna is 

d=2RH

⇒Area =πd2=π×2×R×h=2×6400×98×10-3×π=3940 km2

subject: Physics
q: A signal of 5 kHz frequency is amplitude modulated on a carrier wave of frequency 2 MHz. The frequencies of the resultant signal is/are:
question: A signal of 5 kHz frequency is amplitude modulated on a carrier wave of frequency 2 MHz. The frequencies of the resultant signal is/are:
solution:
Frequency of signal wave =5 kHz=f. Carrier wave frequency =2MHz

=2000kHz=fc. The resultant signal maximum frequency=f+fc

= 5 + 2 0 0 0 kHz

= 2 0 0 5 kHz

Resultant signal minimum frequency

=fc-f

= 2 0 0 0 - 5 kHz

= 1995 kHz

q: A carrier wave of amplitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> is modulated by a sinusoidal base band signal of amplitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The ratio of maximum amplitude to minimum amplitude in an amplitude modulated wave is
question: A carrier wave of amplitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> is modulated by a sinusoidal base band signal of amplitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The ratio of maximum amplitude to minimum amplitude in an amplitude modulated wave is
solution:
The given data is 

Ac=15 VAm=3 V

Thus, the maximum amplitude and minimum amplitude is 

Amax=Ac+AmAmin=Ac-Am

Hence, the ratio is 

AmaxAmin=15+315-3=1812=32

q: A message signal of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kHz</mi></math> and peak voltage of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> is used to modulate a carrier wave of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MHz</mi></math> and peak voltage of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. Then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>a</mi><mi>b</mi></mfrac></math> is_____.
question: The amplitude of upper and lower side bands of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>M</mi></math> wave where a carrier signal with frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>11</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>21</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MHz</mi><mo>,</mo></math> peak voltage <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> is amplitude modulated by a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>7</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kHz</mi></math> sine wave of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> amplitude are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>a</mi><mn>10</mn></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>b</mi><mn>10</mn></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> respectively. Then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>a</mi><mi>b</mi></mfrac></math> is_____.
solution:
In radio communications, a sideband is a band of frequencies higher than or lower than the carrier frequency, that are the result of the modulation process. The signal components above the carrier frequency constitute the upper sideband (USB), and those below the carrier frequency constitute the lower sideband (LSB).

a10=b10=μAC2
⇒ab=1

q: In a communication system operating at wavelength <math><mn>800</mn><mi> </mi><mi>n</mi><mi>m</mi><mo>,</mo></math> only one percent of source frequency is available as signal bandwidth. The number of channels accommodated for transmitting <math><mi>T</mi><mi>V</mi></math> signals of band width <math><mn>6</mn><mi> </mi><mi>M</mi><mi>H</mi><mi>z</mi></math> are (Take velocity of light <math><mi>c</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>8</mn></mrow></msup><mi> </mi><mrow><mrow><mi>m</mi></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><mi>s</mi></mrow></mrow><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mi>h</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>6.6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>34</mn></mrow></msup><mi> </mi><mi>J</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">-</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"><mi>s</mi></mi></math> )
question: In a communication system operating at wavelength <math><mn>800</mn><mi> </mi><mi>n</mi><mi>m</mi><mo>,</mo></math> only one percent of source frequency is available as signal bandwidth. The number of channels accommodated for transmitting <math><mi>T</mi><mi>V</mi></math> signals of band width <math><mn>6</mn><mi> </mi><mi>M</mi><mi>H</mi><mi>z</mi></math> are (Take velocity of light <math><mi>c</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>8</mn></mrow></msup><mi> </mi><mrow><mrow><mi>m</mi></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><mi>s</mi></mrow></mrow><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mi>h</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>6.6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>34</mn></mrow></msup><mi> </mi><mi>J</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">-</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"><mi>s</mi></mi></math> )
solution:
Available signal width =1100×cλ

=1100×3×108800×10-9 Hz=3.75×1012 Hz

Number of available channels =3.75×10126×106

=6.25×105

The correct amplitude modulated<math><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mo>)</mo></math> signal is:

The correct amplitude modulated<math><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mo>)</mo></math> signal is:
options: <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">ν</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mo>⁡</mo><mo>[</mo><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">ω</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><mn>0.01</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">ω</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mo>]</mo></math>

<math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">ν</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mo>+</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></mrow></mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">ω</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">ω</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></math>

<math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">ν</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">ω</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mo>+</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">ω</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></math>
solution:
ym=A cosωt
yc=V0 sinω0t
Modulated signal =V0+A cosωtsinω0t
=V0sinω0t+Acosωt sinω0t
=V0sinω0t+A2sinω0-ωt+A2sinω0+ωt

subject: Physics
q: Choose the correct statement:
question: Choose the correct statement:
options: In frequency modulation the amplitude of the high frequency carrier wave is made to vary in proportion to the amplitude of the audio signal.
In frequency modulation the amplitude of the high frequency carrier wave is made to vary in proportion to the frequency of the audio signal.
In amplitude modulation the amplitude of the high frequency carrier wave is made to vary in proportion to the amplitude of the audio signal.
In amplitude modulation the frequency of the high frequency carrier wave is made to vary in proportion to the amplitude of the audio signal.
solution: As per properties of A.M. in amplitude modulation the amplitude of high frequency carrier wave is made to vary in proportion to the amplitude of the audio signal.

subject: Physics
q: To double the covering range of a TV transmitting tower, its height should be multiplied by:
question: To double the covering range of a TV transmitting tower, its height should be multiplied by:
solution:
The range of a tower in the communication system is given by

Range=2hR

Here, h= Height of the tower,

R= radius of Earth

Given, The range of tower is to be doubled.

As, Range ∝h, to make the range double, the height h have to be made 4 times.
